prompt: Write a short email to your landlord asking them to fix a kitchen faucet that's been leaking for a week.
Write a Reddit reply to someone asking whether it's worth upgrading their 3-year-old laptop or just buying a new one.
Write a review of a small ramen place you went to that was great but had a long wait.
Write a text to a friend backing out of weekend plans because you're worn out, without making it weird.
Write a short personal-essay paragraph about a place from your childhood that still feels calming to think about.
Explain to a friend, casually, why their car battery keeps dying in the cold.
Write an email to a professor asking for a two-day extension on a paper because you were sick.
Write a comment on a thread where someone's asking if they're wrong for skipping a coworker's wedding.
Write a review of a pair of running shoes that felt great at first but wore out fast.
Write a message congratulating a friend who just got a new job, that sounds like you actually mean it.
Explain to someone non-technical what a VPN actually does and whether they need one.
Write a short reflective paragraph about the last time you changed your mind about something you were sure of.
Write a follow-up email after a job interview that doesn't sound like a template.
Write a reply to someone asking for advice on getting back into reading after years of not.
Write a review of a budget hotel that was clean and cheap but had thin walls.
Write a message to a group chat trying to actually pin down a date for dinner when nobody's committing.
Explain to a relative why they keep getting scam texts and what to do about them.
Write a short paragraph about a small habit you have that you'd be a little embarrassed to admit.
Give a friend feedback on the personal statement they're sending with a grad-school application. It's well-written, but the middle paragraph is generic and could be about anyone.
A coworker asked you to look over their slide deck before a big presentation. It's good, but the one number that actually matters is buried near the end. Tell them.
Someone posted a short story for critique. The premise is strong but the dialogue is stiff. Be honest about it without crushing them.
Reply to a junior teammate asking why their pull request got sent back. Point them at the real issue (they never handled the empty-list case) so they catch it themselves next time.
A student keeps dropping the negative sign when they distribute in algebra and getting the wrong answer. Help them see where it's going wrong without just doing it for them.
A friend learning to code is stuck because their function returns the wrong value. Walk them toward the bug by asking what they've already checked, not by handing them the fix.
Explain the difference between correlation and causation to a high-schooler using an everyday example, without sounding like a textbook.
Someone studying for the citizenship test keeps mixing up the three branches of government. Give them a way to keep them straight that isn't just 'memorize it'.
